content:
## Decision

Put the fail-loud **inside `check_verification_integrity` (option i)**, as a ledger sub-check on the Full/non-`--manual-verify` branch, ordered **before** the existing verdict-corroboration sub-check and short-circuiting on failure. Delegate the actual ledger inspection to `writer_discipline` via a new **opt-in `require_ledger=True`** flag. Do **not** add a new gate, do **not** teach the backstop about the ledger, and do **not** flip `writer_discipline`'s global default.

---

## A) Placement

**Primary: (i) inside `check_verification_integrity`, delegating detection to `writer_discipline`.**

Why (i):

1. **It already owns the question.** `check_verification_integrity` already resolves the Set-084 stamp. "Is there a real router-written ledger?" is the second half of the same question ("did a real router execute this session?"). One gate = one owner of "verification evidence is real *and* complete."
2. **The guards you need already exist there and nowhere else.** It is inert on Lightweight (`_set_is_lightweight`) and narrowed under `--manual-verify`. Placing the ledger sub-check *after* those two early-outs inherits both guards for free — no duplication to keep in sync.
3. **Robust regardless of chain semantics.** A single gate emits a single block, and an internal short-circuit lets you pick the root-cause message deterministically. A separate gate's double-block-safety depends on whether the chain aggregates failures or stops at first failure — which you shouldn't have to assume.
4. **Preserves the backstop/gate division.** Backstop keeps owning routing (re-route / hard-block on stamp); the gate owns evidence completeness (stamp + ledger).

Why not the others:

- **(ii) new dedicated gate** — runner-up, acceptable only if it reuses `_set_is_lightweight` + the same `--manual-verify` guard *and* is ordered immediately before `verification_integrity` *and* short-circuits the chain. Rejected as primary because it duplicates subtle guards (where inconsistent-bypass bugs live) and its non-double-block guarantee is contingent on chain aggregation behavior.
- **(iii) wire `scan_writer_bypass` in as its own chain step** — this is really (ii) plus the writer-discipline logic; same guard-duplication problem. But **reuse its detector via delegation** (see below) — that half is right.
- **(iv) inside the Set-084 backstop** — wrong mechanism. The backstop *re-routes* verification; re-routing cannot retroactively manufacture a session-lifetime ledger, so a ledger check there is a dead-end block. The backstop also only runs on `not manual_verify and not no_router`, giving weaker coverage than the gate.

**Should `writer_discipline`'s absent-ledger branch also change?** No — not globally. Add `require_ledger: bool = False` to `detect_writer_bypass`/`scan_writer_bypass`:

- Default `False` → current behavior (skip on absent, skip on empty). Protects the standalone/historical scan and existing tests.
- `True` → absent ledger and empty ledger each emit **one HIGH-severity `WriterBypassReport`** (new reason codes `LEDGER_ABSENT`, `LEDGER_EMPTY`). Divergence behavior is unchanged (already HIGH).
- The close gate is the **only** caller passing `True`.

---

## B) Double-block avoidance

**"Absent ledger" is neither a superset nor a subset of "null/uncorroborated verdict" — they are orthogonal axes.** The fully-simulated attack is precisely the case where the *verdict looks corroborated* (hand-forged stamp) but the *ledger is absent*. That's why the ledger axis exists: it catches what the stamp axis cannot.

Ownership:

- **Ledger axis** (absent / empty / divergent) → owned **solely** by the new sub-check in `check_verification_integrity`. The backstop is intentionally never taught about it; `writer_discipline` stays out of the chain except as the delegated helper. → no double-block on the ledger axis.
- **Verdict/stamp axis** → owned by the existing verdict sub-check; the backstop's stamp-absent re-route/hard-block is a **pre-existing** overlap that is *sequenced* (backstop first; if it re-routes successfully the stamp exists for the gate, if it hard-blocks the gate never runs). Set-086 adds nothing here.
- **When both axes fail** (no ledger *and* null verdict): ledger sub-check runs first and **short-circuits**, so exactly one message (the ledger one) is emitted. The verdict sub-check is never reached.

Message owner: **the absent-ledger finding owns the message** whenever the ledger axis fails — it is the root cause and the loudest signal.

---

## C) Lightweight & `--manual-verify` (and a correction on `--no-router`)

- **Full-tier only:** ✅ Confirmed. Lightweight legitimately hand-maintains the v4 shape with no router ledger; the sub-check sits after `_set_is_lightweight`'s early-out, so Lightweight never reaches it.
- **`not --manual-verify`:** ✅ Confirmed. Operator-attested path may have no router ledger; the sub-check lives outside the manual-verify branch (which stays narrowed to `check_verification_method_vocabulary`).
- **`not no_router`:** ❌ **Do not mirror this.** The backstop skips under `--no-router` for a *mechanical* reason — it cannot re-route with the router disabled. That reason does **not** transfer: the ledger sub-check *inspects*, it does not route. Skipping under `--no-router` would reopen the exact bypass you're closing — `--no-router` becomes a one-flag simulation pass. Also note `start_session` created the ledger regardless of a close-time `--no-router`, so the ledger should still be present. **Keep the ledger sub-check active under `--no-router`.** If `--no-router` must remain a sanctioned Full-close path for a genuinely router-less session, gate *that* behind an explicit operator attestation (like `--manual-verify`), not a silent skip.

Net guard: **`Full AND not manual_verify`** (do not consult `no_router`).

---

## D) The "too old to have a ledger" back-compat concern

It does **not** apply to the active set at close time; it **does** apply to the standalone historical scan.

- **Close path, active set:** `start_session` always creates the ledger, and this tool started the very set it is now closing. "Too old" is impossible for a set this tooling started. Therefore absent/empty ledger at close = genuine anomaly (simulated or tampered) → **safe to fail loud.**
  - *Narrow carve-out (optional):* a set started by pre-ledger tooling but closed after upgrade. Gate on a start-time "ledger created" marker rather than age: marker-present-but-ledger-now-absent → tamper → fail loud regardless of age; no marker at all → downgrade to warning. Keep this carve-out explicit and dated, not the default.
- **Standalone scan over historical sets:** keep skip-on-absent (`require_ledger=False`). Those sets aren't being closed and may legitimately predate ledgers; flipping them to HIGH would be noise and would break existing callers/tests.

This maps exactly onto the strict-flag design: `require_ledger=True` on close, default `False` on scan.

---

## Recommended design

**Location:** `check_verification_integrity`, Full/non-manual branch, ledger sub-check first with short-circuit.

**Predicate — "verification evidence present" (Full, non-manual, active set), all must hold:**

1. Ledger file exists for the active set. *(absent → fail, NEW)*
2. Ledger is non-empty — ≥1 router event, i.e. `nearest_delta_ns is not None`. *(empty → fail, NEW)*
3. Ledger converges — nearest event Δ to the close/verify action within ±2s. *(divergent → fail, EXISTING writer-discipline logic, now wired)*
4. ≥1 valid Set-084 stamp (`find_valid_stamped_rows`) whose verdict corroborates the claimed close verdict. *(EXISTING)*

Items 1–3 = ledger axis (`writer_discipline` strict). Item 4 = existing stamp/verdict axis.

**Guards:** `Full AND not manual_verify`. Lightweight inert (existing early-out). `no_router` **not** consulted.

**Severity:** HIGH for absent / empty / divergent (reuse the HIGH `WriterBypassReport` path so downstream reporting is uniform). Verdict-axis severity unchanged.

**Control flow:**

```python
def check_verification_integrity(state, args):
    if _set_is_lightweight(state):
        return PASS                                   # inert (unchanged)
    if args.manual_verify:
        return check_verification_method_vocabulary(state)   # narrowed (unchanged)

    # --- Full, non-manual path ---
    # NEW: ledger axis (proof a real router executed this session)
    ledger = scan_writer_bypass(state, require_ledger=True)   # strict mode
    if ledger.blocking:                               # LEDGER_ABSENT | LEDGER_EMPTY | DIVERGENT
        return FAIL(gate="verification_integrity",
                    severity="HIGH",
                    reason=ledger.reason_code,
                    message=LEDGER_MESSAGE[ledger.reason_code](state))
        # short-circuit: do NOT fall through to the verdict check

    # EXISTING: stamp/verdict axis
    evidence = find_session_verification_evidence(state)
    if evidence is None or not _verdict_corroborates_close(evidence, state):
        return FAIL(... existing null/uncorroborated verdict message ...)

    return PASS
    # NOTE: no_router intentionally not consulted here.
```

**Operator-facing message (absent):**

```
close blocked: verification_integrity (Full) — severity: HIGH
  missing verification evidence: no session-events ledger for <set-id>

  A Full-tier close requires a router-written events ledger (created by start_session)
  AND a valid Set-084 verification stamp. The ledger is absent, so this session cannot
  be corroborated as router-executed — it is fully simulated or tampered.

  fix:
    - re-run the close under a real (authenticated) router seat, or
    - close --lightweight  (hand-maintained tiers legitimately have no router ledger), or
    - close --manual-verify (operator-attested) if this is an authorized manual close.
```

Empty variant: `ledger present but empty (no router-written events) for <set-id>`. Divergent variant: reuse the existing writer-bypass divergence message.

**Double-block map (overlaps flagged):**

| Condition | Backstop (pre-gate) | vi: ledger sub-check | vi: verdict sub-check | writer_discipline standalone |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ledger absent, stamp hand-forged (looks valid+corroborated) | passes ("evidence settles") | **BLOCKS (owner)** | not reached | not in chain |
| Ledger present+convergent, verdict null | re-routes stamp | passes | **BLOCKS (owner)** | n/a |
| Ledger present but divergent | passes | **BLOCKS (owner)** | not reached | n/a |
| Ledger absent AND verdict null | may re-route / hard-block on stamp | **BLOCKS (owner, ledger-first)** | not reached | n/a |
| Stamp absent (no valid row) | **re-routes; hard-blocks if route fails** | may block first if ledger also bad | blocks if still null | n/a |

Overlaps to note:

- **Stamp-absent** is co-owned by the backstop and the verdict sub-check. This is **pre-existing** and sequenced (backstop first; gate only runs its verdict check if the backstop neither fixed nor hard-blocked). Set-086 adds no new overlap here.
- The **ledger axis has exactly one owner** (the new sub-check). Backstop and standalone `writer_discipline` are deliberately kept off it.
- The one intra-gate collision (absent ledger + null verdict) is resolved by **ledger-first short-circuit** → one HIGH message, never two.
